bullets: TCP uses a sliding window for flow control so a fast sender never overwhelms a slow receiver.
Congestion control uses Slow Start and AIMD to probe and back off from network congestion.
Reliability comes from checksums, ACKs, and retransmission (including Fast Retransmit/Recovery).

slides.title: Why SCTP Exists — TCP's Two Problems
slides.heading: Why was SCTP Created?
slides.subheading: Two Problems TCP Could Not Solve

content.text: Problem 1 — Head-of-Line (HOL) Blocking: TCP delivers data as one ordered stream. If one segment is lost, all segments behind it wait — even if they already arrived correctly. Example: three images are requested; image 2's packet is lost, so image 3 is stuck waiting even though it arrived fine.

Problem 2 — Single Path Dependency: TCP binds a connection to one IP address per side. If that path fails, the entire connection drops and must restart. This is critical in telecom signaling, where availability cannot be interrupted.

slides.analogy: TCP is a single-lane road — one broken-down car blocks everyone. SCTP opens multiple lanes; a blockage in one lane does not stop the others.
slides.visual_guidance: Left: TCP — one stream, one path, a blocked queue. Right: SCTP — 3 independent streams, 2 paths (Path A primary, Path B standby).
slides.speaker_notes: TCP has two limitations that matter in telecom. First, head-of-line blocking — one lost packet holds up everything behind it. Second, single path dependency — if the network path fails, the connection dies completely. SCTP was built to fix exactly these two problems.

slides.title: SCTP — What It Is & Key Features
slides.heading: SCTP — Stream Control Transmission Protocol

content.text: SCTP is a connection-oriented, reliable Transport Layer protocol — like TCP, but with two extra capabilities.

items: Multi-Streaming: one association carries multiple independent streams; a loss in Stream 1 does NOT block Stream 2 or 3. Each stream has its own Stream Sequence Number (SSN).

Multi-Homing: both endpoints register multiple IP addresses at setup. One is the Primary Path; others are Standby. Heartbeat chunks monitor standbys; if the primary fails, SCTP switches automatically with no reconnection.

slides.analogy: Multi-Streaming is like a WhatsApp chat sending a photo, a voice note, and a text at once — if the photo stalls, the text and voice note still go through. Multi-Homing is like your phone on both WiFi and mobile data — drop WiFi and the call silently continues on mobile data.
slides.visual_guidance: Top: SCTP Association -> 3 streams; Stream 1 'Retransmitting', Streams 2 & 3 'Delivering'. Bottom: two hosts with 2 IPs each — Path 1 failed, Path 2 active.
slides.speaker_notes: SCTP sits at the Transport Layer alongside TCP and UDP. Its connection is called an Association; data units are Chunks. Multi-Streaming means one association runs multiple independent streams, so a lost packet in one does not affect others. Multi-Homing means both sides register backup IPs and SCTP silently switches on failure. This is what makes SCTP invaluable in 5G and telecom networks.

slides.title: SCTP 4-Way Handshake & Comparison
slides.heading: SCTP Handshake and Where It Sits

rows: Request association; share IPs & stream count

content.text: Why the Cookie? The server allocates no resources until the cookie is echoed back, so fake INIT messages cannot exhaust server memory — built-in protection against a SYN-flood style DoS attack.

slides.analogy: Like a clinic that first hands you a token slip and only opens a file once you return with it — people who never come back cost the clinic nothing.
slides.visual_guidance: Ladder diagram for the 4-way handshake with a note at Step 2: 'Server stores no state yet.' Below it, the comparison table with the SCTP column highlighted.
slides.speaker_notes: The extra step in the 4-way handshake is the Cookie. The server sends a stateless token and only commits resources when the client echoes it back — a clean protection against SYN-flood attacks. Compared side by side, SCTP keeps TCP's reliability while adding multi-streaming and multi-homing that neither TCP nor UDP offer.

slides.title: What is QoS & Its Parameters
slides.heading: Quality of Service (QoS)

content.text: On the same network a video call needs smooth, real-time delivery — delay kills it — while a background software update can wait a few seconds. Without QoS the network treats both identically and the video call suffers.
QoS = the ability of a network to manage traffic and give different levels of service to different data types, so critical, time-sensitive apps like voice and video get the resources they need.

rows: Available data rate on a path

slides.analogy: Think of a hospital emergency room — a heart-attack patient is seen immediately while a minor cut waits. QoS gives urgent network traffic the same priority.
slides.visual_guidance: Router in the centre; three traffic types entering — Video Call (red), Software Update (blue), Streaming (green). The video call gets a wider output lane. Label: 'QoS — Priority-Based Service.'
slides.speaker_notes: QoS recognises that not all traffic has the same urgency. A video call cannot tolerate delay or jitter — even 200 ms one-way makes a call feel unnatural. A background download has no such need. The four parameters to remember are delay, jitter, bandwidth, and packet loss.

slides.title: QoS Techniques — Scheduling & Traffic Shaping
slides.heading: QoS Techniques

content.text: A — Scheduling: who gets served first at the router queue?

rows: First in, first out. No priority.
Each queue gets a % share (Voice 50%, Video 30%, Data 20%)
One packet per queue in turns

content.text: B — Traffic Shaping: control the rate before data enters the network.

slides.analogy: Priority Queuing is First Class boarding the plane first; WFQ reserves a number of seats for each class so no one is left out. Leaky Bucket is a water tank with a small hole — pour too fast and it overflows (packets dropped).
slides.visual_guidance: Two diagrams — Leaky Bucket (bursty in, steady drip out) and Token Bucket (tokens accumulating, a burst of packets released).
slides.speaker_notes: Scheduling decides priority at the router queue — WFQ is the practical standard. Traffic Shaping controls the rate before data enters the network. Leaky Bucket enforces a strict constant rate and drops excess. Token Bucket is more lenient — save tokens and send a burst, but the average rate is still capped.

sections.name: IntServ, DiffServ & QoS in Switched Networks

slides.title: Best Effort — The Default Behaviour
slides.heading: Best Effort
slides.subheading: How the Internet Works by Default

content.text: By default the internet makes no guarantee about how or when a packet is delivered — this is Best Effort delivery.

items: No priority — every packet is treated equally.
No reservation — no resources are set aside.
No guarantee — packets may be delayed, dropped, or delivered out of order.
Fine for email, downloads, browsing; fails for live video, VoIP, real-time apps.

slides.analogy: A general compartment in a train — no reservation, no guarantee. If there is space you sit; if not, you stand.
slides.visual_guidance: A router with many identical packets entering, no priority lanes, some packets dropped. Label: 'Best Effort — No Guarantee.'
slides.speaker_notes: Before IntServ and DiffServ, understand the default — Best Effort. No priority, no reservation, no guarantee. This is exactly why IntServ and DiffServ were introduced for time-sensitive traffic.

slides.title: IntServ & DiffServ
slides.heading: Two Approaches to Network-Wide QoS

content.text: IntServ (per-flow reservation): the application reserves resources along the whole path before sending, using RSVP (PATH downstream, RESV upstream, each router reserving). Problem: every router stores state for every flow — not scalable.
DiffServ (per-class treatment): no per-flow tracking. The edge router marks each packet with a 6-bit DSCP value in the IP header; core routers only read the label and forward — highly scalable.

slides.analogy: IntServ is a reserved train seat booked before the journey — guaranteed but rigid. DiffServ is a highway with Express, Fast, and Slow lanes — each vehicle is assigned a lane by class.
slides.visual_guidance: Left — IntServ: PATH/RESV ladder, each router 'Reserves Bandwidth'. Right — DiffServ: edge router marks DSCP -> core routers read label -> three output lanes (EF, AF, BE).
slides.speaker_notes: IntServ reserves bandwidth per flow at every router — hard guarantees but it cannot scale to millions of flows. DiffServ marks packets with a class label at the edge and core routers just forward by label — no state, fully scalable. DiffServ runs enterprise and ISP networks today.

slides.title: QoS in Switched Networks
slides.heading: QoS in Switched Networks (Local Network)

content.text: QoS also works inside a local network — your college, office, or data centre — using Ethernet switches and the IEEE 802.1p standard.

items: Every Ethernet frame can carry a VLAN tag (802.1Q).
Inside the tag is a 3-bit PCP (Priority Code Point) field = 8 levels (0 lowest to 7 highest).
A VoIP packet is tagged PCP = 6; a file download PCP = 0. The switch serves higher-priority frames first.

slides.analogy: A busy school canteen with a separate express counter for teachers — teachers (high-priority frames) are served first while students wait in the regular queue.
slides.visual_guidance: An Ethernet frame with the 802.1Q VLAN tag highlighted; zoom into the 3-bit PCP field. Next to it a switch with two queues — High Priority and Normal.
slides.speaker_notes: QoS is not just for wide-area networks — Ethernet switches use a 3-bit PCP field inside the VLAN tag, giving 8 priority levels. VoIP and video are tagged high and served first; a file download gets priority 0 and waits. This keeps voice and video smooth on a busy office network.

key_takeaways: SCTP is a reliable, connection-oriented transport protocol (RFC 4960) using Associations and Chunks.
Multi-Streaming solves HOL blocking; Multi-Homing solves single-path failure.
SCTP's 4-way handshake adds a Cookie for built-in DoS protection.
QoS manages traffic via four parameters: delay, jitter, bandwidth, and packet loss.
Scheduling (WFQ) and Traffic Shaping (Leaky/Token Bucket) are the core QoS techniques.
Best Effort, IntServ (RSVP, per-flow), and DiffServ (DSCP, per-class) are the three network-wide QoS models.
Switched networks use IEEE 802.1p PCP bits in the VLAN tag to prioritise traffic locally.
